heroOverview: Craniorachischisis is the most severe form of neural tube defect, occurring when the embryonic neural tube fails to close along its entire length — producing an open skull at the head combined with an open spine extending down the full length of the vertebral column. The entire neural axis is exposed and unfused, which distinguishes it from anencephaly (head only) or spina bifida (spine only). It is uniformly lethal: virtually all affected fetuses are stillborn or survive only minutes to hours after delivery. The birth prevalence is estimated at 1–2 per 100,000 pregnancies, making it considerably rarer than isolated anencephaly. Because the defect encompasses the total neural tube, it is thought to result from very early failure of the initial axial fusion point rather than from multiple independent closure failures.

keyStats.prevalence: Estimated 1–2 per 100,000 pregnancies; represents fewer than 2% of all neural tube defects (Copp et al., 2003)
keyStats.demographics: Affects fetuses of all ethnicities; female predominance, similar to other NTDs
keyStats.avgAge: Defect originates in the first 21–26 days post-conception; detected prenatally from the first trimester
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global counts are unknown due to early pregnancy loss; estimated several thousand cases annually worldwide

definition: Craniorachischisis (ICD-10: Q00.1) is a severe congenital malformation of the central nervous system in which the neural tube fails to close along its entire craniospinal axis, resulting in a combined open skull defect and open spinal column. The cerebral hemispheres, calvarium, and scalp are absent — identical to anencephaly — and the entire dorsal spine is unfused, with the spinal cord exposed and unprotected from the base of the skull to the sacrum. Unlike spina bifida, in which only a segment of the spine is open, craniorachischisis involves complete axial disruption. Current models suggest it results from failure of the earliest neural fold fusion event (closure point 1 in the mouse model analogue) rather than from multiple independent closure failures. It belongs to the group of open NTDs along with anencephaly and myelomeningocele, and shares the same primary environmental risk factor — periconceptional folate deficiency — as well as genetic susceptibility involving planar cell polarity (PCP) pathway genes including VANGL1, VANGL2, CELSR1, and SCRIB. The condition is universally fatal; no therapeutic intervention can restore the absent or exposed neural tissue.

typesClassification.description: The most severe form, with failure of the entire neural tube from the cranial to sacral level; no segment of the spine or skull is closed, and no region of the neural tube is protected.

Rare variants in which the open defect involves the cranium and an extensive but not necessarily complete spinal segment; the boundary with extensive anencephaly plus spina bifida can be difficult to define.

primarySymptoms: Absence of the calvarium (skull bones above the orbits) visible on first-trimester ultrasound from 11 weeks, identical to the acrania of anencephaly.
Open spinal column along the entire dorsal surface, visible on ultrasound as absence of the normal posterior spinal arch closure and exposed hypoechoic neural tissue.
Absent cerebral hemispheres with only brainstem remnant, producing the same 'frog-eye' sign as isolated anencephaly.
Severe polyhydramnios in the third trimester, caused by absent swallowing reflex secondary to absent cortical function.
Markedly elevated maternal serum alpha-fetoprotein (MSAFP), typically higher than isolated anencephaly because of the larger area of exposed neural tissue.
Elevated amniotic fluid acetylcholinesterase, positive for open NTD.
Grossly abnormal fetal posture on ultrasound — the fetus may appear curled or locked in fixed positions due to absent cortical motor control and spinal cord disruption.
Complete absence of voluntary fetal movement or responses on fetal behavioural assessment.
Stillbirth in the vast majority; any liveborn infant shows only primitive brainstem reflexes.
Absent normal Moro, rooting, and grasp reflexes in liveborn neonates.
earlyWarningSigns: Markedly elevated maternal serum AFP at first second-trimester screen, often higher than seen in isolated anencephaly.
Absent fetal cranial vault combined with absent posterior spinal arch closure on first-trimester nuchal translucency ultrasound.
Grossly abnormal cranial and spinal appearances at the routine anomaly scan at 18–22 weeks.
Failure to identify any normal posterior fossa structures or spinal canal on targeted fetal anatomy survey.
emergencySigns: Severe polyhydramnios causing maternal respiratory compromise, preterm labour, or uterine overdistension requiring urgent obstetric review.
Spontaneous preterm labour in a pregnancy known to be affected, requiring co-ordinated obstetric and palliative care team response.
Malpresentation with obstructed labour requiring prompt obstetric intervention.
Maternal sepsis from prolonged rupture of membranes in a pregnancy complicated by polyhydramnios.

causes.cause: Failure of neural fold fusion at closure point 1
causes.description: In normal neurulation, the neural folds first fuse at a midpoint along the dorsal axis (closure point 1) and then zipper in both directions. Failure at this earliest fusion point — estimated to occur around day 20–21 — prevents any subsequent closure, leaving the entire axis open. PCP pathway gene variants are strongly implicated in this specific failure.

Mutations in VANGL1, VANGL2, CELSR1, and SCRIB disrupt the directional cellular movements (convergent extension) required for neural fold elevation and apposition. These are the best-characterised genetic causes of craniorachischisis in mouse models, and human variants have been identified in NTD patients.

Low folate intake around conception impairs one-carbon metabolism required for DNA synthesis and neuroepithelial proliferation. As with all NTDs, folate deficiency is the principal modifiable risk factor; however, some PCP-pathway-driven cases may be relatively folate-resistant.

Hyperglycaemia in weeks 3–6 of gestation disrupts oxidative stress regulation in the neuroepithelium and increases the risk of all open NTDs by 2–3-fold, acting through mechanisms partly independent of folate.

Valproic acid in early pregnancy raises total NTD risk approximately 10–20-fold; the specific contribution to craniorachischisis is difficult to quantify due to its rarity but is expected to follow the same pattern as other NTDs.

In mouse models, inositol depletion produced by certain anticonvulsants or dietary restriction causes craniorachischisis. Human epidemiological evidence is limited but supports a possible role for myoinositol in neural tube closure independent of folate.

riskFactors.description: The most actionable risk factor for all NTDs including craniorachischisis. Women not taking folic acid before conception have significantly higher risk; the absolute protective effect of supplementation is established across multiple RCTs and large observational studies.

Variants in planar cell polarity genes are the best-established genetic risk factor specific to craniorachischisis, with heterozygous carriers at elevated risk; full penetrance requires additional hits, consistent with a multifactorial model.
A prior NTD-affected pregnancy raises recurrence risk for any NTD to 2–5%; the recurrence may be the same type or a different NTD variant.
2–3-fold elevated NTD risk associated with periconceptional hyperglycaemia; tight control (HbA1c below 6.5%) before and in early pregnancy substantially reduces but does not eliminate the risk.

riskFactors.factor: Valproate or other antifolate drug use
riskFactors.description: Among the highest-risk teratogenic exposures for NTDs; safer anticonvulsant alternatives should be considered for women of childbearing age.

Drugs that deplete myoinositol — including valproate at high doses — may impair a folate-independent closure pathway; myoinositol supplementation is under investigation as an additional preventive measure.

riskFactors.factor: Low socioeconomic status and food insecurity
riskFactors.description: Poor access to folate-rich foods and prenatal supplements increases risk; populations without mandatory grain fortification have persistently higher NTD rates.

affectedDemographics: Fetuses of all ethnicities, with higher rates in populations lacking mandatory folic acid fortification
Female fetuses at a higher rate than male, consistent with other NTDs
Pregnancies in women with pre-existing diabetes
Fetuses in families carrying PCP pathway gene variants
Pregnancies exposed to valproate or other antifolate teratogens in the first four weeks
diagnosisOverview: Craniorachischisis is diagnosed prenatally by ultrasonography. From 11 weeks of gestation, first-trimester scanning reveals absence of the fetal calvarium combined with failure of the dorsal spinal arches to close along the full or near-full length of the spine — a combination not seen in isolated anencephaly or isolated spina bifida. The second-trimester anomaly scan at 18–22 weeks confirms the full extent of the defect. Maternal serum AFP is elevated to levels typically exceeding those seen in isolated anencephaly, because the entire neural tube surface area is exposed to amniotic fluid. Amniocentesis confirms markedly elevated amniotic fluid AFP and positive acetylcholinesterase. Fetal karyotype or chromosomal microarray is offered to exclude co-existing chromosomal anomalies. Detailed anatomy assessment looks for associated cardiac, renal, and limb abnormalities. Genetic counselling discusses the role of PCP pathway gene sequencing, particularly when there is no obvious environmental explanation. Because craniorachischisis can resemble severe anencephaly with associated scoliosis, careful spinal survey is essential to distinguish it from isolated anencephaly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies both absent calvarium (acrania) and absent posterior spinal arch closure simultaneously, establishing the combined craniospinal nature of the defect.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Transabdominal or transvaginal scan; the sonographer surveys the head and the entire spine in longitudinal and transverse planes. The combined appearance of absent skull and unfused spine is unmistakable at this gestational age.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Maps the full extent of the spinal defect, assesses for associated structural anomalies, and measures amniotic fluid volume.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A comprehensive fetal anatomy survey performed by a maternal-fetal medicine specialist or senior sonographer; results are discussed with the family at the time of the scan.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies AFP leakage from the extensive exposed neural tissue; values are typically very high, often exceeding 10 multiples of the median.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine blood draw from the maternal arm; results available within a few days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the open NTD with high specificity via acetylcholinesterase, and allows chromosomal analysis to identify co-existing abnormalities.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A fine needle is passed through the maternal abdomen under ultrasound guidance; mild cramping is expected; chromosomal results take 10–14 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Excludes trisomy 18, trisomy 13, or other chromosomal causes in the minority of cases where a chromosomal anomaly co-exists.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on amniotic fluid or chorionic villus sample; turnaround 7–14 days for microarray.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies heritable variants in VANGL1, VANGL2, CELSR1, or SCRIB that carry recurrence implications for the family and may guide targeted genetic counselling.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Sent from amniotic fluid or postnatal tissue; results typically in 3–6 weeks; interpretation requires clinical geneticist review.

treatmentOverview: Craniorachischisis is universally and rapidly fatal; no surgical or medical intervention can repair the absent and degenerated neural tissue. Management after diagnosis is entirely directed at the family's needs and wishes. Options include continuation of pregnancy with a structured perinatal palliative care plan, induction of labour at a gestational age chosen by the parents, or termination of pregnancy where legally available. Families who continue the pregnancy are supported by a multidisciplinary team — maternal-fetal medicine, neonatology, palliative care, social work, and genetics — to plan a dignified birth, with the goals of comfort for the infant and supported time together for the family. Obstetric complications including severe polyhydramnios may require amnioreduction to relieve maternal respiratory distress; prolonged pregnancy due to absent fetal adrenal function may require induction at term. After delivery, neonatal management is purely comfort-focused: warmth, gentle handling, symptom relief. Resuscitation is not appropriate given the complete absence of any capacity for conscious neurological function. Genetic counselling is essential before any future pregnancy: high-dose folic acid (4–5 mg daily) should begin at least 3 months before conception, and PCP gene results inform targeted testing of future embryos or fetuses if requested.

medicalTreatments.name: High-dose periconceptional folic acid (prevention in next pregnancy)
medicalTreatments.description: Women with a prior NTD-affected pregnancy, including craniorachischisis, are advised to take 4–5 mg of folic acid daily beginning at least one month and ideally three months before the next conception, continuing through the first trimester.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces recurrence risk of any NTD by approximately 70% versus no supplementation, based on the MRC Vitamin Study and multiple subsequent trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Amnioreduction for severe polyhydramnios
medicalTreatments.description: Serial ultrasound-guided withdrawal of amniotic fluid to relieve maternal dyspnoea, abdominal pressure, and risk of preterm labour from uterine overdistension in affected pregnancies that continue.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Provides effective symptomatic relief in approximately 80–90% of procedures; does not alter fetal prognosis.

medicalTreatments.name: Perinatal palliative care programme
medicalTreatments.description: An interdisciplinary, pre-birth care plan developed with the family that specifies the location of delivery, resuscitation decisions, comfort measures for the neonate, family support, and bereavement care. Guided by neonatology, palliative care, and midwifery teams.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Families accessing structured palliative care programmes report significantly better bereavement outcomes in published quality-of-care studies; approximately 90% rate care as good or excellent.

medicalTreatments.name: Myoinositol supplementation (investigational)
medicalTreatments.description: Myoinositol at 1 g twice daily alongside folic acid is being trialled as an additional NTD preventive agent, particularly for cases potentially driven by PCP pathway disruption that may be relatively folate-resistant. Not yet standard of care.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Preliminary data from the FASSTT and myoinositol trials show a trend toward additional NTD risk reduction; larger trials are ongoing.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary folate optimisation
alternativeTreatments.description: Maximising intake of folate-rich foods — dark leafy greens, legumes, fortified cereals — is complementary to supplementation but cannot reliably deliver the 4–5 mg dose required for women with prior NTD-affected pregnancies.

alternativeTreatments.name: Pre-conception genetic counselling and carrier testing
alternativeTreatments.description: PCP gene panel testing of both parents allows identification of heritable variants and enables pre-implantation genetic testing in families who wish to pursue this option in future pregnancies.

whySeeSpecialist: As soon as craniorachischisis is suspected on ultrasound, referral to a maternal-fetal medicine specialist is essential for confirmation, detailed anatomical survey, and management planning. A clinical geneticist or genetic counsellor should be involved to assess recurrence risk, discuss PCP gene testing, and provide pre-pregnancy planning advice. Neonatology and palliative care teams should be engaged in advance of delivery when the family chooses to continue the pregnancy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Care should be centralised at a tertiary fetal medicine unit with expertise in lethal fetal anomalies, offering on-site genetic counselling, amniocentesis, microarray, and a formally structured perinatal palliative care programme. Ask whether the centre has a named bereavement support team and whether perinatal post-mortems are available for families who wish them, as post-mortem findings can refine recurrence risk estimates.
preventionStrategies: Take 400 mcg of folic acid daily starting at least one month before conception; after a prior NTD, increase to 4–5 mg daily.
Eat folate-rich foods daily — dark leafy vegetables, legumes, fortified cereals.
Achieve tight glycaemic control before conception if you have diabetes.
Discuss safer anticonvulsant options with your neurologist before planning a pregnancy if you are on valproate.
Ask about myoinositol supplementation if you have a prior NTD-affected pregnancy or a known PCP gene variant — emerging evidence suggests it may provide additional protection beyond folate alone.
Support population-level mandatory folic acid food fortification policies.
lifestyleModifications: Start a prenatal vitamin with 400–800 mcg of folic acid before stopping contraception.
Maintain a diet rich in leafy greens, beans, and fortified grains throughout the periconceptional period.
Control blood glucose tightly if you have diabetes.
Avoid fever without treatment in weeks 3–8 of pregnancy; treat pyrexia promptly with paracetamol.
Avoid alcohol and smoking, both of which impair folate status.

recommended: Dark leafy greens such as spinach, kale, and broccoli, rich in natural folate
Legumes including lentils, chickpeas, and black beans, among the most folate-dense foods
Fortified breakfast cereals providing 100% of the recommended daily folate allowance
Oranges and citrus fruits as an accessible folate source
avoid: Excessive alcohol, which reduces folate absorption and raises plasma homocysteine
Liver in large amounts during early pregnancy (excess retinol is teratogenic at high doses)
Unpasteurised soft cheeses and raw meat (general early pregnancy food safety)

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate exercise is safe and encouraged before and during pregnancy, and does not raise NTD risk. Avoid saunas, hot tubs, and hot yoga during weeks 3–8 of the first trimester because of hyperthermia risk. Pregnant women in a craniorachischisis-affected pregnancy should follow general obstetric activity guidelines, with modification for polyhydramnios if present.
dailyManagement: Families continuing the pregnancy should work with the fetal medicine and palliative care teams to complete a detailed birth plan well before the expected delivery date.
Monitor for signs of severe polyhydramnios — rapid abdominal enlargement, breathlessness — and report promptly for assessment.
Attend all scheduled fetal medicine appointments, as interval growth monitoring and fluid assessment guide obstetric decisions.
Engage with psychological support and bereavement counselling services early; waiting lists for specialist services can be long.
Begin planning the next pregnancy with a clinical geneticist, including discussion of PCP gene testing and the recommended folic acid dose.
prognosis: Craniorachischisis is universally lethal. The vast majority of affected fetuses are stillborn; of those born alive, survival is typically measured in minutes to hours. No case of meaningful long-term survival has been documented. For families, the prognosis for future pregnancies is substantially improved by early genetic counselling, high-dose folic acid supplementation, optimisation of modifiable risk factors, and, where available, pre-implantation genetic testing for families with identified PCP gene variants. Psychological outcomes for parents are improved by access to specialist perinatal palliative care and bereavement support services.
recoveryTimeline: There is no recovery for the infant. For parents, acute grief is typically most intense in the first weeks to months following loss. Most describe gradual adaptation over 1–2 years, though anniversaries, subsequent pregnancies, and milestones can trigger renewed grief. A minority experience prolonged grief disorder requiring clinical intervention. Most families can begin planning the next pregnancy physiologically after one menstrual cycle, though emotionally, a waiting period of several months to a year is common and appropriate.
complications: Severe polyhydramnios — present in a higher proportion of craniorachischisis pregnancies than isolated anencephaly, due to the greater extent of exposed neural tissue and more complete loss of fetal swallowing; risk of maternal respiratory distress and preterm labour.
Malpresentation and obstructed labour — the abnormal fetal form, combined with possible kyphoscoliosis, increases the risk of face, brow, or transverse lie during labour.
Prolonged pregnancy — absent hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis function delays the hormonal cascade that normally triggers labour onset.
Maternal psychological morbidity — clinically significant depression, anxiety, and PTSD affect 20–30% of mothers following loss from a lethal structural anomaly.
Recurrence risk — without high-dose periconceptional folic acid, recurrence risk for any NTD is 2–5% after one affected pregnancy.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anencephaly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anencephaly involves only the anterior neural tube failure (absent brain and skull), without an open spine. Craniorachischisis combines anencephaly with a fully open spinal column.

confusedWithConditions.name: Spina bifida
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Spina bifida involves focal posterior neural tube failure at one or more spinal segments, with an intact skull; craniorachischisis involves the entire craniospinal axis simultaneously.

confusedWithConditions.name: Encephalocele
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Encephalocele is a herniation of brain tissue through a focal skull defect with an otherwise intact calvarium and no spinal involvement; it has a far better prognosis than craniorachischisis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Iniencephaly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Iniencephaly involves retroflexion of the head and neck with occipital bone defect and cervical spinal anomaly, but it does not involve full-length open neural tube; it is less extensive than craniorachischisis.

relatedConditions.name: Anencephaly
relatedConditions.relevance: The anterior-only form of the same neural tube closure failure; craniorachischisis is effectively anencephaly plus total spinal dysraphism, and shares all major risk factors.

relatedConditions.name: Iniencephaly
relatedConditions.relevance: Another lethal NTD involving the occiput and cervical spine; related by shared embryological origin and risk factor profile.

relatedConditions.name: Spina bifida
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common NTD, involving only the posterior spinal neural tube; shares the same modifiable risk factors including folate deficiency and valproate exposure.

relatedConditions.name: Encephalocele
relatedConditions.relevance: A milder NTD variant involving focal brain herniation through a skull defect; part of the spectrum of neural tube closure failures.

relatedConditions.name: Microcephaly
relatedConditions.relevance: Another major prenatal brain malformation detected on ultrasound requiring specialist fetal medicine and genetic assessment.

faqs.question: What is craniorachischisis?
faqs.answer: Craniorachischisis is the most severe neural tube defect, where the entire neural tube from head to spine fails to close in early pregnancy. The result is an absent skull, absent brain, and an open spine along its full length. It is uniformly fatal.

faqs.question: How is craniorachischisis different from anencephaly?
faqs.answer: Anencephaly involves failure of only the head end of the neural tube, leaving the spine intact. Craniorachischisis involves failure of the entire neural tube — both the head and the full length of the spine — making it a more extensive and equally fatal defect.

faqs.question: Is craniorachischisis fatal?
faqs.answer: Yes, universally. Nearly all affected fetuses are stillborn. Of the rare infants born alive, survival is typically limited to minutes or hours. No treatment can restore the absent or degenerated neural tissue.

faqs.question: What causes craniorachischisis?
faqs.answer: The defect results from failure of the earliest neural fold fusion event in the embryo, around day 20–21 post-conception. Key risk factors are periconceptional folate deficiency, variants in planar cell polarity genes (VANGL1, VANGL2, CELSR1), maternal diabetes, and valproate use during early pregnancy.

faqs.question: Can craniorachischisis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Risk can be significantly reduced with 400 mcg of folic acid daily before conception. Women with a prior affected pregnancy should take 4–5 mg daily, which reduces NTD recurrence risk by approximately 70%. Tight glycaemic control, avoiding valproate, and avoiding fever in early pregnancy are also recommended.

faqs.question: How is craniorachischisis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: It is diagnosed prenatally on ultrasound. From 11 weeks, the scan shows both absent skull and an unfused open spine along the full vertebral column. These features are confirmed at the 18–22 week anomaly scan. Maternal serum AFP is markedly elevated; amniocentesis adds acetylcholinesterase confirmation and chromosomal analysis.

faqs.question: What is the recurrence risk after craniorachischisis?
faqs.answer: After one NTD-affected pregnancy, the recurrence risk for any NTD is approximately 2–5%. High-dose folic acid (4–5 mg daily) before the next conception reduces this risk by roughly 70%. Genetic testing for PCP pathway variants may provide additional information for recurrence counselling.

faqs.question: What genes are associated with craniorachischisis?
faqs.answer: Planar cell polarity (PCP) pathway genes — VANGL1, VANGL2, CELSR1, and SCRIB — are the best-established genetic causes. These genes control the directional cell movements needed for neural fold closure. Most cases are multifactorial, however, combining genetic susceptibility with environmental factors such as low folate.

faqs.question: What options do parents have when craniorachischisis is diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Parents may choose to continue the pregnancy with perinatal palliative care — a care plan focused on comfort and family time after birth — or to end the pregnancy through induction of labour or termination where legally available. There is no corrective treatment. Genetic counselling is provided for all families.

faqs.question: Is craniorachischisis the same as spina bifida?
faqs.answer: No. Spina bifida involves failure of only a segment of the posterior spinal neural tube, with an intact skull, and variable neurological function. Craniorachischisis involves the entire craniospinal neural tube, producing a combined absent skull, absent brain, and fully open spine — always fatal.

faqs.question: How rare is craniorachischisis?
faqs.answer: It is estimated to affect 1–2 per 100,000 pregnancies, representing fewer than 2% of all neural tube defects. It is much rarer than isolated anencephaly or spina bifida.

faqs.question: Can craniorachischisis be seen on a first-trimester scan?
faqs.answer: Yes. From approximately 11 weeks of gestation, an experienced sonographer can identify the combined absence of the skull and open spinal column that defines craniorachischisis. It is one of the most structurally obvious fetal anomalies visible on first-trimester ultrasound.

faqs.question: Does craniorachischisis run in families?
faqs.answer: There is a genetic component: PCP pathway gene variants — including VANGL1 and VANGL2 — are heritable and have been identified in families with multiple NTD-affected pregnancies. After one affected pregnancy, recurrence risk rises to 2–5% for any NTD. Genetic counselling and testing is recommended for all affected families.

faqs.question: What is the role of myoinositol in craniorachischisis prevention?
faqs.answer: In mouse models, myoinositol depletion specifically causes craniorachischisis, and supplementation prevents it. In humans, early trials suggest myoinositol alongside folic acid may provide additional protection, particularly for women who have had a prior NTD unexplained by folate deficiency alone. It is not yet standard of care but is under active investigation.

faqs.question: What happens to the mother during a craniorachischisis pregnancy?
faqs.answer: The mother's health is usually not directly endangered by the fetal condition, but polyhydramnios — too much amniotic fluid — can develop and cause abdominal distension, breathlessness, and preterm labour risk. Prolonged pregnancy may also occur. Serial obstetric monitoring and possible amnioreduction are part of ongoing care.

faqs.question: Is craniorachischisis caused by chromosomal abnormality?
faqs.answer: Most cases are not caused by chromosomal abnormalities, but trisomy 18 or trisomy 13 are found in about 15–20% of all NTDs including craniorachischisis. Chromosomal analysis via amniocentesis or microarray is offered to all families to identify co-existing abnormalities.

faqs.question: What is the difference between craniorachischisis and encephalocele?
faqs.answer: Encephalocele is herniation of brain tissue through a localised skull defect, with an intact calvarium and no spinal involvement; it is compatible with survival. Craniorachischisis involves total absence of the skull and full-length open spine — it is always fatal and structurally far more extensive.

faqs.question: Can craniorachischisis be treated with surgery?
faqs.answer: No. Unlike some forms of spina bifida, where fetal surgery can improve outcomes, craniorachischisis cannot be repaired surgically. The absent brain tissue cannot be restored, and there is no neural function to preserve. Management is entirely palliative and family-support focused.

faqs.question: What support is available for families affected by craniorachischisis?
faqs.answer: Specialist perinatal palliative care teams, bereavement midwives, and genetic counsellors are available at most tertiary fetal medicine centres. Peer support groups for families who have experienced NTD losses exist in many countries. Psychological support — including trauma-focused therapy — is available and recommended for parents experiencing significant grief or PTSD after loss.

searchTags: open spine and skull birth defect
metaDescription: Craniorachischisis is a lethal NTD where both the skull and entire spine fail to close in pregnancy. Learn about causes, diagnosis, and folic acid prevention.
keywords: open neural tube head and spine
